title: Maps
summary: The technology of representing spatial relationships on a surface — one of the oldest cognitive and political tools in human history, a technology that does not merely describe territory but creates it: setting borders, asserting sovereignty, enabling navigation, organizing colonial space, and shaping how people understand their place in the world.
hook: Every map is an argument. The Mercator projection — still the default in most online mapping applications — makes Europe and North America appear much larger than they are and Africa much smaller. This is not an error: it was a useful distortion for navigation. But when it became the default image of the world in schools and offices, it encoded a geography of relative importance that had nothing to do with area. Maps have always been made from somewhere by someone for some purpose. Understanding them requires asking who made them, who they were made for, and what they were made to do.

beginner.theFoundation:
Pull up a map on your phone, and the world looks settled. Africa looks about the size of Greenland. Borders run as crisp colored lines, as if someone traced what was already there.

But that picture is misleading. Africa is roughly fourteen times larger than Greenland. The phone map stretches the far north and shrinks the middle of the globe. That happens because the Earth is a ball, and a ball cannot be flattened without bending something.

So every map is built from choices. What goes in. What gets left out. Where the center sits. Which place gets a name, and which stays blank. These choices are rarely just technical. They show what the mapmaker cared about and who held the power.

Maps are very old. One of the oldest is a clay tablet from Babylon, made around 2,600 years ago. Almost every early civilization made them, from Egyptian land records to Pacific stick charts. For as long as people have drawn the world, they have used those drawings to claim it.
beginner.howItArrived:
Around 500 years ago, mapmaking in Europe changed fast. Scholars rediscovered an old Greek book by a writer named Ptolemy. It gave them math tricks for drawing a round world on a flat page. Meanwhile, Spanish and Portuguese ships were bringing back fresh details about distant coasts.

Within about a hundred years, European mapmakers had drawn rough but usable maps of the whole world, including the Americas. People often tell this story as steady progress. That story leaves out almost everyone else.

Chinese mapmakers were already skilled. Islamic scholars had drawn world maps more careful than the European ones of their day. Pacific navigators crossed open ocean by reading stars and waves. Mapping was not a European invention. Europe's maps spread widely later because its ships and armies did, not because the maps were better.

In 1569, a mapmaker named Gerardus Mercator made a flat world map that kept compass directions straight. That made it handy for sailing, and it slowly became the world map most people pictured.
beginner.whatItReorganized:
A map can change what a country even is. Long ago, rulers thought less about exact borders and more about people. A king held power over villages, lords, and families who owed him loyalty. The edges of his land stayed fuzzy.

Modern maps made the edges sharp. Once a clear line was drawn, a government could point at it and say everything inside belongs to us. The line turned a loose patch of land into a single ruled space.

This got used as a weapon. When European powers wanted to claim faraway places, they drew maps of those places first, sometimes before anyone had traveled there. They wrote 'unknown land' across the blank parts and stamped their own names onto the rest. Drawing the map became a way of claiming it.
beginner.thePoliticalEconomy:
Some of history's most powerful maps are land records, called cadastral maps. They mark exactly who owns which piece of ground, so they decide who pays taxes and who can be pushed off their land.

In England, over several centuries, shared village fields were fenced off and handed to private owners. Surveyors mapped those fields first, which made them easy to carve up. In colonized parts of Africa, the Americas, and Asia, officials drew maps labeling land already lived on as 'empty.' Calling it empty made taking it look fair.

The same logic still runs in our own time. The boundary on the official map decides who owns what. If your land is not on that map, the government may not see it as yours. People left off the map can lose everything to people on it.
beginner.theFeedback:
Maps do not just record the world. They teach people what it looks like, and the picture sticks.

Think of a map on a classroom wall. When a country draws its own map, it usually shows every scrap of land it claims, even land that neighbors say is not theirs. China's maps stretch a line deep into the South China Sea that nearby nations reject. India and Pakistan draw the border in Kashmir in different places. Children grow up trusting whichever version hung in their school.

This is why the shape of a map can start an argument. The old Mercator map, made for sailing, puffs up places near the top and bottom of the globe. Europe looks big. Africa looks small. A map that keeps sizes honest shows Africa is actually larger than the United States, China, and Europe put together. The choice looks like math. The effect is power.
beginner.presentAndFuture:
By the 2020s most maps lived on screens. Google Maps, GPS, and satellite photos have made map data sharper and easier to reach than before. But there is a catch. The maps almost everyone uses now belong to private companies that make money from ads and deliveries, not from serving the public.

Some people use these same digital tools to push back. Local and native communities draw their own maps to show land they have long used, sacred places, and boundaries that official maps ignore. When a disaster strikes, volunteers can map roads and buildings within hours to help rescuers.

Going digital did not settle the old questions, though. Who gets to make the map? Who is it for? Whose version of a place counts as correct? Those questions are as alive on a screen as they were on paper.

general.theFoundation:
Open the map app on your phone and Greenland looks about the size of Africa. In reality Africa could swallow Greenland fourteen times over. The map is not broken. It leans on a 16th-century projection built for sailors, which stretches the far north and squeezes the tropics to keep compass lines straight.

That distortion is the door into a larger claim: maps are not innocent pictures of the world. They are arguments about it. A map decides what to show and what to omit, where to place the center, and which settlements deserve a name. Those decisions carry the values and interests of whoever drew it.

This way of reading maps is often called critical cartography, and one influential statement of it came from the geographer J.B. Harley in the late 1980s. Harley argued that maps exercise power as much through their silences as their contents. An unnamed village, an erased indigenous place name, a boundary quietly drawn or left off: these absences can be as political as anything printed in bold.

The view has critics. Some argue it treats maps too narrowly as instruments of power and undervalues a real achievement, the ability to represent coastlines and distances with genuine precision. That tension, between maps as honest knowledge and maps as quiet persuasion, still animates the field.
general.howItArrived:
The familiar story of mapmaking runs from crude medieval sketches to precise modern charts, a tidy march of European progress. The wider record complicates it. Around 500 years ago European cartography did transform quickly, helped by the rediscovery of Ptolemy's Geography, which supplied mathematics for projecting a sphere onto a flat page, and by data streaming back from Iberian voyages. Within roughly a century, European mapmakers had drawn workable world maps that included the Americas.

But sophisticated traditions already existed elsewhere. Al-Idrisi's world map of 1154, made for Roger II of Sicily, captured the Old World more accurately than its European contemporaries. It was drawn with south at the top, reflecting a different orientation rooted in the Islamic world, and it folded in knowledge from trade networks reaching across the Indian Ocean toward West Africa.

The historian Jerry Brotton has traced this plural history through a sequence of landmark maps, from Ptolemy to digital globes, each one carrying the assumptions of the society that made it. Chinese, Islamic, Pacific, and European traditions had their own centers, orientations, and purposes. Europe's conventions later spread across the globe largely because European ships and empires did, not because the maps alone won on accuracy. Mercator's 1569 projection, useful for navigation, gradually became the default mental image of the world.
general.whatItReorganized:
Maps did not just describe states. They helped build them. Before modern cartography, political authority ran through people more than territory: webs of lords, vassals, tribute, and allegiance, with edges that blurred and shifted. The modern idea of a state ruling everything inside a fixed line was made thinkable, and enforceable, by maps that showed precisely where that line ran.

Colonial surveys reveal the process clearly. The Survey of India, carried out over several decades from the late 18th century, was at once a rigorous scientific feat, producing accurate maps through painstaking triangulation, and an assertion of British control through systematic measurement. The historian Matthew Edney has argued that the survey did not simply picture British India. It helped call that India into being, turning a varied subcontinent into a single unit that imperial administrators could govern as one.

The wider point is that many modern borders were not traced around realities that already existed. They were drawn, and the drawing made a place administrable and defensible. This holds for states born after empire too: a great many of their borders descend from colonial lines sketched with little regard for the communities living across them.
general.thePoliticalEconomy:
If maps once helped seize land, they can also help defend it. A movement sometimes called counter-mapping turns community cartography into a tool for asserting claims that official maps ignore. In Indonesia, researchers documented how villagers mapped their customary use of forests, use that state forestry maps had rendered invisible, then carried those maps into land disputes with the government. In Canada and the United States, indigenous groups have used maps of long historical use as evidence in treaty and land-rights cases.

This shows that the techniques of measuring, bounding, and representing are not owned by the powerful. The same tools that enabled dispossession can be turned to resist it. Yet counter-mapping runs into a hard limit. To count in court, an indigenous map usually has to speak the legal system's language, which means discrete parcels, written titles, and coordinate grids, even when those forms flatten or distort the spatial relationships a community actually lives by. Winning recognition can require accepting the framework you set out to challenge.
general.theFeedback:
Because maps teach people what the world looks like, the way a map is drawn becomes a political act in itself. The clearest case is the projection fight of the 1970s and 1980s. Arno Peters promoted an equal-area projection he called fair to all peoples, arguing that the Mercator's swollen north encoded a Europe-centered view in the world's default image. The argument found traction, and several development and UN-linked organizations adopted his map.

Professional cartographers pushed back on technical grounds. Peters's projection had its own distortions, stretching tropical regions vertically, and the Mercator had been built for navigation rather than for comparing areas. Still, the quarrel exposed a real question the field had dodged: if every flat map distorts something, what makes one distortion acceptable rather than another, and who gets to decide? Saying that accuracy on the relevant measure should win is reasonable, yet it quietly sidesteps the harder issue of whose relevant measure counts. A map on a wall keeps making its argument long after anyone remembers choosing it.
general.presentAndFuture:
Digital mapping has reshaped who can make a map. OpenStreetMap, a collaborative open project begun in 2004, lets anyone add roads, buildings, and footpaths. In parts of the world that commercial services neglect, its coverage can exceed Google's, because local contributors map what global companies do not find worth the effort. Volunteer teams have built emergency infrastructure maps within hours of disasters in Haiti, the Philippines, and elsewhere.

But crowdsourcing inherits the biases of its crowd. Coverage is dense in wealthy cities and thin across poorer and rural areas, simply because that is where contributors cluster. Old disputes resurface in new form: whether the water between Japan and Korea is labeled the Sea of Japan or the East Sea, whether a contested island appears under one nation's name or another's. When contributors from rival countries disagree about basic geography, governing a shared global map becomes genuinely hard. As of the 2020s, it remains unsolved. The questions cartography has long raised, about who maps, for whom, and whose geography counts, have followed us onto the screen intact.

advanced.theFoundation: Denis Wood's The Power of Maps (1992) extends Harley's analysis through detailed examination of a single map to show how maps present the historical — products of specific human choices — as the natural, the inevitable. The boundary on the map looks like a feature of the landscape rather than a political decision. Maps naturalize social constructions by presenting them as spatial facts.
advanced.howItArrived: The relationship between cartography and the Scientific Revolution — how mathematical geography contributed to the broader transformation of European knowledge — has been analyzed by David Turnbull and Steven Harris. The standardization of measurement, development of accurate projection mathematics, and use of surveys all involved the same epistemological commitments: quantification, systematic observation, reproducibility. But cartography had political and economic drivers other sciences lacked.
advanced.whatItReorganized: The Selden Map — a Chinese map of maritime Asia, probably produced in the early 17th century and rediscovered in the Bodleian Library in 2008 — illustrates how different cartographic traditions construct space differently. Centered on China's southern coast with trade routes as organizing principle, it reflects a Chinese maritime commercial perspective rather than a European territorial sovereignty perspective. Timothy Brook's Mr. Selden's Map of China uses it to analyze how maps reveal not just geography but cosmology.
advanced.thePoliticalEconomy: The contemporary geopolitics of digital mapping shows that cartographic politics have not been resolved by digitization. Google Maps displays different borders in different countries: in India, Kashmir appears entirely Indian; in Pakistan, it shows the Line of Control. China requires all map services to use an offset coordinate system (GCJ-02), making foreign mapping applications inaccurate within China. The politics of digital cartography are the old politics of sovereignty played out in coordinate systems and API protocols.
advanced.theFeedback: The field of indigenous cartography — documentation and recovery of pre-colonial spatial knowledge — has both scholarly and political dimensions. The History of Cartography project challenged the Eurocentric narrative that systematic cartography was a European invention. But the project also raised questions about what counts as a 'map' — whether oral geographical knowledge, stick charts, and memory maps qualify — and whose criteria of inclusion are applied.
advanced.presentAndFuture: Volunteered geographic information (VGI) — spatial data contributed through GPS, mobile phones, and social media — has transformed the data landscape. OpenStreetMap contains local detail government and commercial maps lack in many regions. But VGI data has systematic biases: it overrepresents wealthy, urban, English-speaking populations. The gap between the ubiquity of digital spatial data and its unequal representation is one of the defining equity challenges in contemporary geography.

foundation.theStory: Maps matter as one of the oldest and most consequential technologies in human history — tools that enable navigation, organize political territory, assert sovereignty, and shape how people understand their place in the world. Understanding them requires moving beyond maps as neutral representations to maps as political arguments.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern whether maps are neutral representations or political arguments, the relationship between colonial cartography and colonial possession, how map projections encode cultural biases, whether digital mapping has democratized or concentrated cartographic power, and how indigenous spatial knowledge should be recognized.
foundation.whyItMatters: Maps matter for understanding navigation, colonial expansion, the modern state system, property law, national identity, the Scientific Revolution, and the contemporary politics of territory and borders.
questions: The Mercator projection makes Europe appear larger than Africa. Arno Peters argued this reflected Eurocentric bias; cartographers argued it was a navigational tool being misused. Is a map projection politically biased if it was designed for a different purpose? Who is responsible for the political effects of a technical choice?

Colonial cartography mapped territories before they were explored, asserting ownership through representation. Contemporary international law recognizes territory based partly on historical maps. Does this give historical cartographic claims legal force — and if so, whose historical maps should count?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: J.B. Harley's The New Nature of Maps is the essential foundation of critical cartography. Denis Wood's The Power of Maps is the most accessible account. Jerry Brotton's A History of the World in Twelve Maps provides the accessible narrative history. Timothy Brook's Mr. Selden's Map of China is the best case study. For colonial cartography, Matthew Edney's Mapping an Empire is the standard account.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: Analysis of historical maps requires both technical expertise (understanding projection systems, surveying methods) and interpretive sensitivity (reading maps as cultural artifacts). The main limitation is the fragile and incomplete survival of early maps. For contemporary maps, the primary challenge is understanding the algorithmic and commercial decisions that determine what appears in digital services.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about maps must engage with the critical cartography tradition — taking a position on whether Harley's claim that all maps are political arguments is too strong or too weak. It must address whether colonial cartography constituted colonial possession or merely reflected it. And it must engage with whether digital maps are more or less politically charged than their paper predecessors.
questions: OpenStreetMap allows anyone to contribute to a collaborative world map — but also allows anyone to delete data, insert fake roads, or rename places to assert territorial claims. Does democratizing cartographic production make maps more or less reliable as representations of reality?

China requires all digital maps within its territory to use an offset coordinate system that makes foreign applications inaccurate. This is described as a security measure. Who does this serve, and what does it reveal about the relationship between digital cartography and state sovereignty?

research.topic: The relationship between colonial cartography and territorial possession
research.content: Scholars including Edney, Harley, and the post-colonial geography tradition have established that mapping was both a tool and expression of colonial possession — that placing territory on a European map asserted sovereignty recognized by other European powers, that colonial surveys made territories legible for extraction, and that categories used by colonial maps ('empty,' 'terra nullius') provided legal justifications for indigenous dispossession.

research.topic: The politics and accuracy of digital mapping services
research.content: Political geographers have documented systematic patterns in how Google, Apple, and other commercial mapping services handle contested territories — displaying different borders for different national audiences, handling indigenous place names inconsistently, and prioritizing commercial over public geographic information. The relationship between digital mapping accuracy and social equity — systematic gaps in coverage for low-income and non-English-speaking communities — is also an active research area.

research.topic: The spatial knowledge systems of pre-literate societies
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: systematic documentation of spatial knowledge, navigation methods, and territorial understanding of pre-literate societies comparable in detail to the documentary record for literate civilizations. Why unavailable: pre-literate spatial knowledge was transmitted orally and through practice; it was often suppressed or replaced by colonial education; and people who possessed it were displaced or killed before documentation. What can be established: partial documentation from anthropological fieldwork and surviving communities. What cannot be established: the full extent and sophistication of spatial knowledge systems destroyed by colonial processes.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Progressive Map
comparativeNarrative.content: Scientists and cartographers tend to read the history of mapping as steady progress. Early maps were rough and speculative; modern ones are mathematically precise. On this reading, politics are secondary to accuracy. The Peters projection controversy shows where this view runs into trouble. An equal-area map is more accurate about size but distorts shape. A navigational map is more accurate about compass bearings but distorts area. Every projection is accurate on the measure it was built for. Calling one map better than another requires saying better for what purpose.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Political Map
comparativeNarrative.content: Critics like J.B. Harley argue that maps are political arguments dressed up as neutral pictures. Every map chooses a center, a scale, and what to include or leave out. Those choices encode the values of whoever made the map. An unnamed village, a border drawn to suit a colonial power, a sea labeled with one nation's name and not another's: these are not neutral technical decisions. The key questions are always who made this map, who it was made for, and what it was designed to do.

comparativeNarrative.name: Other Geographies
comparativeNarrative.content: Indigenous traditions challenge the idea that European mathematical cartography sets the standard for all spatial knowledge. Polynesian navigators used stick charts to map ocean swells and currents. Aboriginal Australians encoded geography in songs that could be followed across thousands of miles. Inuit communities held detailed mental maps of coastlines and hunting grounds. These were not lesser maps waiting to be replaced by better ones. They were different tools built for different purposes. Taking them seriously means rethinking the story that mapping history runs in a single direction.

rabbitHole.label: Navigation
rabbitHole.reason: Maps and navigation developed together — the demand for accurate navigational charts drove the development of mathematical cartography, and improved maps enabled more ambitious navigation.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: Colonial cartography was both a tool of colonial expansion and an expression of colonial sovereignty — asserting possession through the act of representation.

rabbitHole.label: Empire
rabbitHole.reason: Every empire has required maps — to administer territory, plan military campaigns, coordinate resource extraction, and assert the spatial extent of sovereign authority.

rabbitHole.label: The Scientific Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: Mathematical cartography — the development of projection systems, geodetic surveying, and coordinate systems — was both a product of and contributor to the Scientific Revolution's commitment to quantification.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: The national map — showing the nation's territory as a bounded, colored space — is one of the most powerful symbols of national identity and a primary tool through which national territory is constructed and contested.

rabbitHole.label: Paper
rabbitHole.reason: Paper enabled reproduction and distribution of maps at scale — before paper and printing, maps were rare expensive objects; after, they could be produced in quantity and widely distributed.

rabbitHole.label: The Silk Road
rabbitHole.reason: The Silk Road's routes were mapped by Arab and Chinese geographers centuries before European cartography — al-Idrisi's world map reflects Islamic geographical knowledge of the trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean.

rabbitHole.label: The Internet
rabbitHole.reason: Digital mapping has transformed how spatial information is produced and accessed; the politics of digital cartography — whose map of a contested territory is 'correct' — are the old politics of sovereignty in new form.

reference.author: Harley, J.B.
reference.title: The New Nature of Maps: Essays in the History of Cartography
reference.annotation: The foundational text of critical cartography — Harley's essays establishing maps as political arguments transformed how geographers and historians analyze cartographic sources.

reference.author: Brotton, Jerry
reference.title: A History of the World in Twelve Maps
reference.annotation: The best accessible history — Brotton's account of twelve maps spanning the ancient world to Google Earth shows how each reveals the values of its era; the ideal starting point for general readers.

reference.author: Wood, Denis
reference.title: The Power of Maps
reference.annotation: The essential critical account — Wood's analysis of how maps exercise power is the most accessible entry into critical cartography.

reference.author: Edney, Matthew
reference.title: Mapping an Empire: The Geographical Construction of British India
reference.annotation: The essential colonial cartography account — Edney shows the inseparability of scientific and political purposes in colonial mapping.

reference.author: Brook, Timothy
reference.title: Mr. Selden's Map of China: Decoding the Secrets of a Vanished Cartographer
reference.annotation: The entry's case study of non-European cartographic space — Brook reconstructs the rediscovered Selden Map to argue it encodes a Chinese maritime commercial cosmology rather than a European territorial-sovereignty perspective.

reference.author: Woodward, David; Harley, J.B. (eds.)
reference.title: The History of Cartography
reference.annotation: The multi-volume scholarly project the entry credits with dismantling the Eurocentric story that systematic mapmaking was a European invention, while raising the question of what counts as a 'map.'

reference.author: Turnbull, David
reference.title: Maps Are Territories: Science Is an Atlas
reference.annotation: Underpins the advanced layer's claim that mathematical cartography shared the epistemological commitments — quantification, systematic observation, reproducibility — of the Scientific Revolution while carrying political and economic drivers other sciences lacked.

reference.author: Ptolemy, Claudius
reference.title: Geography (Geographia)
reference.annotation: The recovered classical text the entry credits with supplying European cartography its projection mathematics for rendering a sphere on a flat page during the rapid transformation of c. 1500.

reference.author: al-Idrisi, Muhammad
reference.title: Tabula Rogeriana (world map for Roger II of Sicily)
reference.annotation: The primary cartographic artifact the entry narrates to show a non-European tradition — drawn with south at the top and more accurate than its European contemporaries — countering the story of mapmaking as European progress.

commerce.title: A History of the World in Twelve Maps
commerce.author: Jerry Brotton
commerce.note: Start here — the most readable cartography history; Brotton shows how each of twelve maps reveals the values of its era; no specialist knowledge required.

commerce.title: The Power of Maps
commerce.author: Denis Wood
commerce.note: The essential critical account — Wood's analysis of how maps exercise power through silences as much as content; more accessible than Harley and the best introduction to critical cartography.

commerce.title: How to Lie with Maps
commerce.author: Mark Monmonier
commerce.note: The essential practical guide — Monmonier's account of how maps distort, mislead, and manipulate teaches you to read any map critically; genuinely enjoyable.

commerce.title: Mr. Selden's Map of China
commerce.author: Timothy Brook
commerce.note: The best case study — Brook uses one rediscovered 17th-century Chinese map to illuminate an entirely different spatial imagination; beautifully written.

commerce.title: Prisoners of Geography
commerce.author: Tim Marshall
commerce.note: The most accessible account of how geography shapes politics — Marshall's analysis of how rivers, mountains, and coasts determine what nations can do; the right entry for readers who want the political science before the cartography.

popularCulture.title: Google Maps and the politics of digital cartography
popularCulture.description: Open Google Maps in India and Kashmir looks fully Indian. Open it in Pakistan and the border shifts. The app shows each country the lines its government demands. Those lines come from political deals, not facts on the ground. The smooth screen hides this. It looks like a window onto one real world, when it is really one version chosen for you.

popularCulture.title: The Map of Tiny Perfect Things — Ian Samuels (2021)
popularCulture.description: This teen film treats mapmaking as a way to pay attention. The two leads chart small, lovely moments most people walk past, building a private atlas of a single day. The film keeps maps gentle and personal. Real maps leave places out on purpose, and those silences carry weight. A village with no name on the page can vanish from how people picture the world.

popularCulture.title: 'Here be dragons' and unknown space
popularCulture.description: The words 'Here be dragons' sit on just one known old map, the Lenox Globe of about 1510. Now the phrase turns up in tech manuals and business talk for any risky, unknown ground. It names that unsettled feeling at the edge of what you know. It also paints medieval mapmakers as wild dreamers. Most of them were careful workers, drawing coasts and ports for trade and travel.

popularCulture.title: The Peters projection debate
popularCulture.description: In the 1970s and 1980s, Arno Peters showed how the common world map swelled Europe and North America while shrinking Africa and South America, planting a quiet message about whose land counted. The Mercator map does stretch size that way. Peters' own map fixed the sizes but warped the shapes, pulling tropical countries long and thin. Every flat map of a round Earth bends something somewhere.

popularCulture.title: Piranesi — Susanna Clarke (2020)
popularCulture.description: In Clarke's novel, the narrator maps a vast, flooded House hall by hall, counting rooms and tides to keep his mind whole. Drawing the place is how he holds on. The book frames every map as private and harmless, a personal comfort. Out in the world, maps also set borders, name owners, and back armies. Who draws the lines has always shaped who holds the ground.

comparativeSummary: A map looks like a plain picture of the world, but the people who study maps cannot agree on what they are actually doing. To scientists, a map is knowledge getting steadily more accurate, and politics is a side issue. To critics like Harley, that neutrality is the trick: every map picks a center, a name, a border, and those choices serve someone. To Polynesian navigators or Aboriginal songlines, the whole contest assumes European math is the standard, when other peoples mapped ocean swells and ancestral routes by entirely different rules. The argument is really about what counts as a map at all.
